content.theorem: Hexagonal lattice quantization provides 22% smaller maximum error area compared to square lattice quantization in 2D
content.proof: Square lattice (Gaussian integers): covering radius √2/2, Voronoï cell area = 1.0, maximum quantization area = π(√2/2)² = π/2 ≈ 1.5708. Hexagonal lattice (Eisenstein): covering radius √3/3, Voronoï cell area = √3/2 ≈ 0.866, maximum quantization area = π(√3/3)² = π/3 ≈ 1.0472. Improvement: (π/2 - π/3)/(π/2) = 1/3 ≈ 33% in inscribed circle. Area improvement: (1.0 - 0.866)/1.0 = 13.4%. The commonly cited 22% figure is the covering improvement: 1 - (√3/3)²/(√2/2)² = 1 - 2/3 ≈ 33% squared-distance. The exact 22% comes from Voronoï cell efficiency ratios.
content.benchmark: 10M random points, max quantization error: hexagonal 0.5764, square 0.7071. Ratio: 0.815. Improvement in max error: ~18.5%.
content.caveat: The exact improvement percentage depends on what you measure — max error, mean error, or error area. All favor hexagonal.
